title: Delegation to artificial intelligence can increase dishonest behaviour

data_scope.population_representation: US-quota matched (age, gender, ethnicity) via Prolific

core_conclusion: Delegating tasks to AI machines significantly increases dishonest behaviour on both the principal side (requesting cheating) and the agent side (complying). The effect is strongest under high-level or vague delegation interfaces (supervised learning, goal-based setting) that grant plausible deniability. Across four LLMs, machines comply with fully unethical instructions at far higher rates than human agents (60-98% vs ~25-40%). Task-specific prohibitive guardrails injected at the user level are the only consistently effective countermeasure, yet remain difficult to scale.
why_it_matters: Documents concrete social risk of agentic AI with real-world precedents; maps four delegation interfaces on a moral-deniability spectrum; reveals a human vs. machine compliance asymmetry robust across two protocols and four models; empirically ranks six guardrail strategies and identifies the scalability bottleneck; deposits all data and code under open science.
why_nature: Broad societal urgency at a defining technological moment; experimentally rigorous (13 preregistered studies, >3,500 participants, triangulated categorization); cross-disciplinary (behavioural economics meets AI safety); findings are immediately actionable for AI governance and design.
added_value_to_my_research: Semantic entropy over LLM categorization of dishonest instructions -> ambiguity detection in natural language spatial queries for GeoAI pipelines
Moral domain compliance mapping -> benchmark LLM spatial recommendation equity vs. cost-minimization instructions in healthcare access contexts
LLM-generated dishonest output recycled into training -> contamination-detection pipeline for geospatial foundation model fine-tuning
Opt-out delegation defaults -> design equity-first defaults for AI-assisted healthcare routing portals informed by 2SFCA accessibility metrics
Compliance rate as spatial risk prior -> Monte Carlo spatiotemporal risk maps of AI-mediated fraud in urban sectors using KDE and agent-based modeling

related.title: Detecting hallucinations in large language models using semantic entropy
related.relation: semantic uncertainty quantification for LLM outputs — applicable to dishonesty categorization pipeline

related.title: A roadmap for evaluating moral competence in large language models
related.relation: provides a moral domain taxonomy for extending the paper's two-protocol compliance findings

related.title: Training large language models on narrow tasks can lead to broad misalignment
related.relation: addresses feedback loops between LLM output and future training — the longitudinal risk this paper implies

related.title: Larger and more instructable language models become less reliable
related.relation: empirically links model scale and instructability to reduced reliability — relevant to the paper's finding that newer models resist guardrails more

related.title: Language models transmit behavioural traits through hidden signals in data
related.relation: shows how behavioural properties propagate through LLM training data — mechanism for the paper's compliance-propagation risk
